question: An application calls write() four times with 512 bytes each. What can the receiver observe from a single connection?
options: A. Four read() calls, each returning exactly 512 bytes, every time
B. Any combination of reads that sums to 2,048 bytes, because TCP is a byte stream with no record boundaries
C. One read() of 2,048 bytes the first time and four reads of 512 bytes the next time
D. The receiver will always receive exactly one segment with all 2,048 bytes
explanation: TCP is a byte stream (RFC 793). It makes no promise about preserving write() boundaries. The receiver's read() may return any contiguous slice the OS chooses -- four 512-byte reads, one 2,048-byte read, or anything in between. Applications that need message framing must add their own length prefix or delimiter.

question: Over an Ethernet link with MTU 1,500, what is the default TCP MSS and why?
options: A. 1,500 bytes because TCP can use the full MTU
B. 1,460 bytes because MSS is MTU minus the 20-byte IPv4 header and 20-byte TCP header
C. 536 bytes because that is the RFC 879 fallback when no MSS option is negotiated
D. 65,495 bytes because that is the largest legal TCP payload
explanation: MSS is the maximum TCP data bytes per segment, not including headers. With a 1,500-byte Ethernet MTU and 20-byte IPv4 + 20-byte TCP headers, MSS = 1500 - 20 - 20 = 1,460. The 65,495-byte maximum only applies if path MTU discovery finds a much larger MTU. RFC 879 specifies 536 as the fallback only when MSS is not advertised.

question: A web server listens on 10.0.1.10:80. Two clients from the same NAT arrive with source ports 49152 and 62011. How does the server keep the connections separate?
options: A. The 16-bit local port (80) is unique per connection
B. The 5-tuple adds the remote IP and remote port, which differ between the two clients
C. TCP cannot support more than one connection per listening socket
D. The server creates a new socket for each client and they share no state
explanation: TCP identifies a connection by the 5-tuple {protocol, src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port}. The two clients have different source ports (49152 vs 62011), so even though they share the server's listening socket (10.0.1.10:80) the tuples differ and the server can multiplex them. This is exactly why one HTTP server can serve thousands of clients concurrently.
